comments.body: @nkrivenko The question is in the header. :)
Sorry no this is for JUnit4

answers.body:
I think you can use EnviromentVariableRule at junit4.

Look at this link: https://www.baeldung.com/java-system-stubs#2-junit-4-environment-variables

It does not seem to be possible to mock System. See here

System Stubs might also be a good alternative for JUnit5

You can create the object in the production code and mock that object in test.

In production code

Object Env{
       fun getVar() = System.getEnv("key")
}

then call with Env in production code

val callObject = Env.getVar()

In test file

@BeforeEach
fun setup() {
mockkObject(Env)
}

In test you can enter this -

every { Env.getVar() } returns "value"

Add the below configuration in maven-surefire-plugin dependency of your pom.xml, and run your junit test case as it is without making explicit changes related to System.getEnv() like Mocking System.class. This is not allowed/required.

If for example below is how your environment variable looks like :-

  • Env Variable name := PROPERTY_NAME
  • Env Variable Value := PROPERTY_VALUE

And if you access that environment variable using the below line Java :- System.getEnv("PROPERTY_NAME");

Make the below changes to your pom.xml :-

<dependency>
  <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
  <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>2.22.2</version>
  <configuration>
   <environmentVariables>
      <PROPERTY_NAME>PROPERTY_VALUE</PROPERTY_NAME>
   </environmentVariables>
  </configuration>
</dependency>

This can be achieved using System Stubs. Add this in POM:

<dependency>
  <groupId>uk.org.webcompere</groupId>
  <artifactId>system-stubs-jupiter</artifactId>
  <version>2.1.6</version>
  <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>

Then use stubs in tests:

@ExtendWith(SystemStubsExtension.class)
class WithEnvironmentVariables {

    @SystemStub
    private EnvironmentVariables variables =
        new EnvironmentVariables("KEY", "VALUE");

    @Test
    void hasAccessToEnvironmentVariables() {
        assertThat(System.getenv("KEY"))
            .isEqualTo("VALUE");
    }

    @Test
    void changeEnvironmentVariablesDuringTest() {
        variables.set("KEY", "bar");

        assertThat(System.getenv("KEY"))
            .isEqualTo("bar");
    }
}

Reference: https://github.com/webcompere/system-stubs?tab=readme-ov-file#quickstart-junit-5

create an EnvVariable class that encapsulates the logic to retrieve environment variables. Then, in the main production code, you can call this class to get the environment variables. This design allows you to easily mock the Env class in your tests.

public class EnvVariable{

// Method to retrieve the environment variable "key"
public String getVar1() {
    return System.getenv("key");
}
}

@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) 
public class TestEnvVariable {

@Mock
EnvVariable
......

Unfortunately you cannot simply mock java.lang.System with Mockito. The workaround is to create a separate class (could be static but will be more complicated) or a helper class.

In my case I utilized org.apache.commons.lang3.SystemUtils.getEnvironmentVariable since I already have it available.

@Test
void testSystemGetenv() {
  try (var mockedSystem = mockStatic(SystemUtils.class)) {
    mockedSystem.when(() ->SystemUtils.getEnvironmentVariable(anyString(),anyString()))
      .thenReturn("abc");
    assertThat(SystemUtils.getEnvironmentVariable("foo", ""))
      .isEqualTo("abc");
  }
}

For this to work you should also exclude the mockito-core dependency and replace it with mockito-inline.

Note if you're using Spring Boot you have @TestPropertySource e.g.

@TestPropertySource(
    properties = {
      "spring.liquibase.enabled=true",
      "spring.liquibase.contexts=local"
    })
class MyTest {
  ...
}

Another approach that's less intrusive is to use SystemStubsExtension which provides the an EnvironmentVariables stub to alter the environment variables.

The test would be

@SystemStub
private EnvironmentVariables environmentVariables;

@Test
void testSystemGetenv() {
  environmentVariables.set("foo", "abc");
  assertThat(System.getenv("foo")).isEqualTo("abc");
}

If you're on JDK 21 you may need to add this, the @{argLine} allows jacoco to add itself back to the plugin

<plugin>
  <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
  <version>3.5.2</version>
  <configuration>
    <argLine>-Dnet.bytebuddy.experimental=true @{argLine}</argLine>
  </configuration>
</plugin>

While JUnit doesn't have a built-in way of mocking environment variables, the JUnit Pioneer project, which is a widely accepted extension for JUnit, does:

@Test
@SetEnvironmentVariable(key = "some variable", value = "new value")
void testSet() {
    assertEquals("new value", System.getenv("some variable"));
}

See the documentation for additional details.

title: How to Mock System.getenv() with JUnit5
body:
I want to mock the System.getenv() method. I found only solutions for JUnit4 and PowerMockito. I use the following dependency:

    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.mockito</groupId>
        <artifactId>mockito-junit-jupiter</artifactId>
        <version>2.23.0</version>
        <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>

Here is my example of the test:

@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) 
public class TestEnvVariable {

  @Mock
  System system;

  @Test
  public void shouldExpandPropertyContentToMatchingSysEnv() throws Exception {
      when(system.getenv("KEY")).thenReturn("VALUE");
      assertEquals("VALUE", "KEY");
  }
}

How to Mock System.getenv() with JUnit5?
